coredata.dc:title: Ice template induced assembly strategy for preparation of 3D porous carbon frameworks from low-cost carbon quantum dots for high-performance lithium-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Feng
Xing, Baolin
Zeng, Huihui
Guo, Hui
Qu, Xiaoxiao
Huang, Guangxu
Cao, Yijun
Li, Peng
Zhang, Chuanxiang
coredata.dc:description: Consider the natural similarities between the structures of coal tar pitch and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the mass-production of low-cost zero-dimensional (0D) CQDs was achieved by using the mild oxidation with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The as-obtained CQDs possesses a uniformly distributed size with a diameter of 4.16 ± 0.46 nm, many oxygen-containing functional groups, distinct crystallinity nature and a production yield of ∼18 wt%. Subsequently, ice-template induced assembly coupled with carbonization strategy reveals a transition from 0D CQDs to 3D porous carbon frameworks. Differing from the well-defined porous carbon, the obtained porous carbon frameworks consist of cross-linked and twisted nanosheets, achieving a well-developed hierarchical porous structure with a reasonable specific surface area and a gradient distribution of meso/micropores. LIBs based on porous carbon frameworks as anode materials show excellent long-cycle performance, as evidenced by reversible capacity of up to 273 mAh·g−1 after 1000 charge-discharge cycles at 2000 mA·g−1. The research emphasizes a green method of preparing CQDs from representative low-value coal tar pitch and the directional transition from 0D CQDs to 3D porous carbon frameworks, providing a promising idea to prepare functional carbon materials and endow them opportunities for LIBs anode.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Consider the natural similarities between the structures of coal tar pitch and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the mass-production of low-cost zero-dimensional (0D) CQDs was achieved by using the mild oxidation with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The as-obtained CQDs possesses a uniformly distributed size with a diameter of 4.16 ± 0.46 nm, many oxygen-containing functional groups, distinct crystallinity nature and a production yield of ∼18 wt%. Subsequently, ice-template induced assembly coupled with carbonization strategy reveals a transition from 0D CQDs to 3D porous carbon frameworks. Differing from the well-defined porous carbon, the obtained porous carbon frameworks consist of cross-linked and twisted nanosheets, achieving a well-developed hierarchical porous structure with a reasonable specific surface area and a gradient distribution of meso/micropores. LIBs based on porous carbon frameworks as anode materials show excellent long-cycle performance, as evidenced by reversible capacity of up to 273 mAh·g−1 after 1000 charge-discharge cycles at 2000 mA·g−1. The research emphasizes a green method of preparing CQDs from representative low-value coal tar pitch and the directional transition from 0D CQDs to 3D porous carbon frameworks, providing a promising idea to prepare functional carbon materials and endow them opportunities for LIBs anode.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have the advantages of ultra-fast charge and discharge rate, excellent cyclic stability, eco-friendly, low cost, and so forth making them the first choice in the new energy era [1]. Nevertheless, the low theoretical capacity of 372 mAh·g−1 of commercialized graphite does not satisfy the urgent demand of next generation energy storage systems. With a view to circumvent these problems, developing novel anode materials is a key to achieving high-electrochemical performance in LIBs. Considerable research has shown that abundant carbon materials with varied structures for instance 0D (carbon dots [2,3], fullerene [4], etc.), 1D (carbon nanotubes [5], carbon fiber [6], etc.), 2D (graphene [7], carbon nanosheets [8], etc.), and 3D (porous carbon frameworks [9]) can be employed as anode materials to improve lithium storage performance. Among them, 3D porous carbon frameworks are widely favored by researchers thanks to the relatively large specific surface area and high electronic conductivity. Until now, two main approaches have been reported to successfully prepare porous carbon frameworks, namely chemical activation technique using NaCl [10] and ZnCl2 [11] as activation agents or templating method using vermiculite [12] and Ni(OH)2 [13] as templates, respectively. Despite the progress being made, the chemical activation is related to demanding reaction conditions and special equipment as well tedious washing process; whereas the templating preparation route usually uses strong acids (e.g. hydrofluoric acid, sulfuric acid, etc.) to remove the template material, meaning that the synthesis route is complicated and environmentally polluting. Therefore, considering practical applications in large scale, a simple, scalable and cost-effective route is required for obtaining 3D porous carbon frameworks for LIBs.

Among carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have generated widespread interest (usually <10 nm in size) [14,15], are widely used in electronic devices [16], photoluminescent materials [17], bio-imaging [18], biosensors [19], and solar cells [20]. Generally, CQDs are prepared using top-down methods from coal-derived material such as carbon nanotubes [21], fullerenes [22], graphene oxide [23], activated carbon [24] and coal [25], and using bottom-up methods from molecular precursors including citric acid [26], glucose [27], as well as ascorbic acid [28]. However, it is essential to synthesize CQDs from cheaper precursors for their versatility in practical applications. Coal and its derivatives are identified as one of the earth abundant and affordable energy sources. Hu et al. [25] reported a typical oxidation route to acquire carbon dots by selective oxidation of organic carbon from anthracite using hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Zhang et al. [29] acquired graphene quantum dots of approximately 3.25 nm in size by nitric acid oxidation using anthracite coal from Ningxia, and the HRTEM showed a clear lattice spacing. A bandgap tunable CQDs were prepared from anthracite by pre-oxidation with K2FeO4 and re-oxidation with H2O2 [30]. Nevertheless, producing CQDs at a large scale and low cost continues to pose a significant challenge. Furthermore, CQDs also have their own inherent defects, which becomes the bottleneck for further application of CQDs-based in energy storage: (1) CQDs are typical 0D structures with good electrical conductivity, but they cannot form an interconnecting conductive network as electrode materials, resulting in severe limitations in overall electrical conductivity and electrochemical stability of electrodes; (2) although CQDs possess a larger specific surface area, they cannot form an effective hierarchical pore structure, resulting in electrode materials with limited space or active sites to store ions and exhibiting a poor rate capability; (3) CQDs are characterized by the unique small size effect, numerous electron-electron hole pairs and abundant surface functional groups, which can improve the compatibility between electrode materials and electrolyte, but their tendency to detach from the collector fluid, leading to inferior battery performance. Therefore, simple routes of CQDs self-assembly or induced assembly are now considered more appropriate for solving the above problems.

Based on that, researchers have achieved great results. Ji's group [31,32] prepared CQDs from a compound of acetone (C3H6O) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sintered it at 800 °C in an argon atmosphere to prepare 3D porous carbon frameworks. In terms of sodium storage, they show an impressive cycle capability. Subsequently, 1D carbon fibers, 2D carbon nanosheets and 3D porous carbon frameworks were successfully assembled by heating CQDs and inexpensive salts (ZnCl2, NiCl2 and CuCl2) at different temperatures, respectively. Similarly, Zhang and co-workers [33–37] obtained graphene quantum dots from bituminous coal using a chemical oxidation method and assembled into hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets, ultramicroporous carbons, carbon nanofiber and activated carbon via template, hydrothermal, and chemical activation methods. Inspiration from the structure of puzzles, Zhang et al. [38] combined carbon dots with nano-CoP, which achieves a highly effective water decomposition. Based on the same concept, Song et al. [39] proposed a reaction pathway and chemical transformation mechanism for the evolution of 0D carbon dots into 2D carbon nanosheets and constructed 2D composite electrocatalytic (Ru1CoP/CDs nanosheets) materials through the splicing of carbon dots building units. They suggest that many suspended carbon dots are produced under a high temperature argon atmosphere, and that the suspended carbon dots then assemble with each other to produce 2D carbon nanosheets. This strategy shows a positive development of carbon dots in the field of electrocatalytic research and gives researchers some inspiration for other applications. However, the precursors and routes for the synthesis of CQDs are highly expensive, which is not conducive to industrial preparation.

In this paper, an extremely simple ice template induced assembly coupled with carbonization strategy was developed to construct unique 3D porous carbon frameworks using coal tar pitch based CQDs prepared by mild oxidation method as precursors. The resulting porous carbon frameworks exhibit a tailor-made 3D morphology, a large specific surface area (121.13 m2·g−1), and a reasonable pore size distribution, which delivers excellent electrochemical performance as anode for LIBs. In particular, the optimized sample (PCF-700) displays remarkable lithium storage performance in terms of high initial discharge capacity (1021 mAh·g−1), long cycle life (402 mAh·g−1 at 100 mA·g−1 after 500 cycles), and high-rate capability (273 mAh·g−1 at 2000 mA·g−1). This work provides a new perspective for the clean and efficient utilization of coal tar pitch and the exploitation of high-performance carbonaceous anode in LIBs.

The coal tar pitch from Henan Zhong Hong Coal Chemical Co., Ltd. was served as the start material and Table 1 shows its proximate and ultimate analyses. The hydrogen peroxide aqueous solution (30 wt%) was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and porous carbon frameworks
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared by a mild oxidation method with H2O2. In a typical procedure, 1 g coal tar pitch powder was added into 150 mL of 30 % H2O2 solution under ultrasonic agitation for 1 h and then refluxed at 100 °C for 2 h. The resulting solution was subjected to purification by passing it through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to separate unreacted coal tar pitch. The excess H2O2 was eliminated by dialyzing the filtrate in deionized water for 72 h with a dialysis bag (MW: 1000 Da), until the pH of the external water became neutral. After freeze-drying for about 36 h, CQDs powder were achieved with a production yield of ∼18 wt%.

The porous carbon frameworks were prepared via conventional carbonization strategy. About 2 g as-prepared CQDs powder was carbonized in a N2 atmosphere at different temperatures (600, 700, 800 °C) for 1 h under at a rate of 5 °C min−1. The resultant samples at diverse heating temperatures were denoted as PCF-600, PCF-700, and PCF-800. The synthesis procedures for CQDs and porous carbon frameworks are displayed in Fig. 1 .

The microstructures and morphologies of the prepared carbon samples were characterized with scanning electron microscope (SEM, Merlin Compact, Germany) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan). The structures and components were gained via X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku SmartLab, Japan), Raman (inVia, Renishaw, UK), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher, USA). The functional group or chemical bond of carbon materials was examined by the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, Bruker Tensor 27). The photoluminescence (PL) and ultraviolet-visible spectra of CQDs were obtained using a Nanolog FL3-2iHR luminescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) and Shimadzu UV-2450 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), respectively. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements were performed using an Autosorb-iQ-MP system (Quantachrome, USA) at 77 K to analyze the porous properties of the samples. The specific surface area was determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, while the pore size distribution was analyzed using a density functional theory (DFT) model.

The electrode sheets were prepared as follows. A homogeneous slurry was prepared by mixing the porous carbon frameworks, carbon black, and polyvinylidene fluoride in N-menthyl-2-pyrrolidinone at a weight ratio of 80:10:10 and stirring the mixture. Subsequently, the homogeneous slurry was coated uniformly on a copper foil and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 10 h. Immediately afterwards, the dried copper foil was then promptly cut into 14 mm diameter disks. Assembling the CR2016 half-cells in a glove box, which was filled with high-purity argon, and H2O and O2 contents were strictly controlled to below 0.1 ppm. The counter electrode, separator, and electrolyte used in the experiment were metal lithium sheet, polypropylene membrane (Celgard 2400), and 1 M LiPF6/EC-DEC (EC: DEC, 1:1, v/v) solution, respectively. The mass loading of the active material for each working electrode was 0.7–1.0 mg cm−2, and the film thickness was about 100 μm. The Shenzhen Neware BTS-4000 battery tester was employed for recording galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) data within a voltage window of 0.01–3.0 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves were collected by the CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China).

A digital picture of the aqueous solution of obtained CQDs under natural light and a UV lamp at 365 nm wavelength is shown in the Fig. 2a. The solution appears bright yellow in color and exhibits strong blue-green fluorescence. Without the aid of stirring or ultrasonic treatment, CQDs can be easily dispersed in water/ethanol. More importantly, the as-obtained CQDs aqueous solution remains stable at room temperature, and no noticeable precipitation is observed even after 1 month (Fig. 2b). The TEM image in Fig. 2c reveals the uniform size and shape distribution of the as-obtained CQDs. Random counting and Gaussian fitting of the diameters of 100 particles give a diameter of 4.16 ± 0.46 nm for CQDs. Further HRTEM observations of CQDs in Fig. 2d exhibit a distinct crystallinity nature with an interplanar distance of around 0.34 nm being assigned to the (002) lattice fringe of graphene. The inset in the lower left of Fig. 2d shows that the crystalline nature of the obtained CQDs is more distinct than those CQDs prepared using biomass and other starting materials, which may be due to the unique aromatic nucleus molecule structure of coal tar pitch. The corresponding FFT image (inset top right in Fig. 2d) displays a hexagonal lattice, also indicating that CQDs are crystalline hexagonal structures [40]. The optical properties are analyzed from CQDs suspension (1 mg mL−1) using UV–Vis and PL. A sharp UV–Vis absorbance peak at approximately 223 nm in connection with π-π* transitions of CC and a weaker peak at approximately 308 nm resulting from the n-π* transition of the CO or CO are shown in Fig. 2e [29]. Fig. 2f shows the PL spectra of CQDs at varied wavelengths, revealing that the optimal excitation wavelength is 340 nm. As with other fluorescent CQDs, an excitation-dependent PL of CQDs is also observed [41,42]. The dependence of the PL on excitation wavelength observed may be attributed to either the distribution of different emission sites on the CQDs or the size distribution of the CQDs themselves [40]. Herein, a possible mechanism for the formation of CQDs from coal tar pitch is as follows: the alkyl chains linking on the edges of the coal tar pitch molecules are selectively oxidized to oxygen-containing groups by H2O2, while the aromatic nucleus in the coal tar pitch molecules is preserved and formed graphite domain of the CQDs [40].

The SEM images (Fig. 3a) show that the coal tar pitch has an irregular size and shape distribution with a size range of 50 μm or less. As shown in Fig. 3b, the freeze-dried CQDs appears to be a “thousand-layer pancake” in North China, they are soft and fragile, and light as feathers. As the SEM image in Fig. 3c shows, the local alignment of the CQDs follows the growth of ice crystals upon cooling and consists of thin sheets. Carbonization temperature is a key factor that determines the micro/nanostructure of the carbon materials. The SEM images (Fig. 3d-l) show the progressive changes in the microstructure of the obtained materials with an increase in the heating temperature from 600 °C to 800 °C. When calcined at 600 °C, the resulting sample has an underdeveloped porous structure (Fig. 3d-f). This could be due to the factor that the conditions for the rapid pyrolysis and self-assembly of carbon atoms to carbon nanosheets are not formed at such relatively low temperature. When the carbonization temperature increases (700 and 800 °C), the as-grown nanosheets exhibited a cross-linked 3D porous carbon framework with the linkage of numerous O-rich functional groups (Fig. 3g-i). Furthermore, the cross-linked and twisted nanosheets and interconnected hierarchical porous structure can be seen more clearly from the TEM (Fig. 3m-o) of PCF-700. Thus, the tailor-made shaggy and 3D porous structure consisting of cross-linked and twisted nanosheets, furnishing more efficient pathways for fast electron transportation, which is important for both increased capacity and rate performance.

As displayed in Fig. 4a, the phase structures of CQDs and porous carbon frameworks are investigated by XRD. The broad (002) diffraction peak observed at around 24° can be attributed to graphite, figuring a disordered stacking structure of CQDs. With the increase in carbonization temperature, many functional groups are effectively removed, and the diffraction peaks of the porous carbon frameworks are significantly enhanced, showing their higher crystallinity [43,44]. To identify the evolution of the microcrystalline phase with pyrolysis temperature, the (002) peaks were simulated by a profile-fitting process (Fig. S1). Each part of the fitting peaks corresponds to a type of carbon phase [45], as listed in Table S1. The (002) peaks shift to higher diffraction angle with carbonization temperature, indicating that d002 (the average interlayer spacing of the graphene sheets) decreases due to an increased relative ordering. High calcination temperatures cause local structural changes from highly disorder to short-range order, and strong interactions between the well-developed graphene layers that form at high temperatures reduce the d002 value. The d002 values are calculated to be 0.370, 0.382, 0.380, and 0.375 nm for CQDs, PCF-600, PCF-700, and PCF-800, respectively, which are much larger than that of graphite (0.335 nm). Such increase in the interlayer spacing is beneficial for Li+ insertion. Furthermore, some sp3 C gradually transform into sp2 C, accounting for 51.20 %, 57.91 %, and 58.94 % in PCF-600, PCF-700, and PCF-800, respectively. Typical Raman spectra of as-made porous carbon frameworks are exhibited in Fig. 4b. In the Raman spectra of carbon materials, peaks lie in ∼1350 and ∼1600 cm−1 representing defects in the carbon atom crystal and the in-plane stretching vibration of sp2 hybridization, which are defined as D and G band, separately. In general, the defect degree of carbon materials is assessed by ratio of the intensity between D and G band [46,47]. In the spectrum of CQDs, ID/IG = 0.77, indicating the graphite carbon domains with ordered structure of as prepared CQDs. With the increase of carbonization temperature, the ID/IG decreases from 0.89 (PCF-600) to 0.82 (PCF-800), which further implies the increase in graphitic ordering and the decline of the presence of defects and disordered sections. This partially graphitized carbon is suitable for Li+ storage because of its high conductivity. The FT-IR spectra of CQDs and porous carbon frameworks are presented in Fig. 4c, which show characteristic functional groups. The CQDs exhibit the presence of oxygen-containing groups, such as CO (alkoxy, ∼1060 cm−1), CO (epoxide/other, ∼1230 cm−1), CO (carboxyl, ∼1410 cm−1), CC (aromatic ring, ∼1620 cm−1), CO (carboxyl/carbonyl, ∼1740 cm−1), and -OH (hydroxyl, ∼3410 cm−1) [48,49]. The above results indicate that the CQDs synthesized via the oxidation route possess abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, endowing them with high solubility and stability in water and other polar solvents [50,51]. The FT-IR intensities of the oxygen-containing functional groups are remarkable reduced after annealing at 600, 700 and 800 °C, indicating the oxygen is effectively removed by carbonization. To learn the elemental content and bonding characteristics, XPS were measured in CQDs and porous carbon frameworks. Fig. 4d exhibits the XPS spectra of C 1s, revealing the presence of CC, CO, and CO at ∼284.4, ∼286.0 and ∼288.7 eV, respectively [52]. The XPS measurements shown in Table 2 indicate that the CQDs are enriched with oxygen-containing functional groups, as evidenced by the calculated C/O atomic ratio of 1.9. After carbonization, porous carbon frameworks present similar low contents of oxygen, with only a minimal presence of C-O-C and CO. Specifically, the percent of sp3 carbon (CO and CO) in the overall carbon decreases from 40 % in CQDs to 25 % in PCF-700; accordingly, the C/O atomic ratio increases to 6.7 for PCF-800. Such results are in accordance with the FT-IR analyses.

To further understand the specific surface area and pore structure features of porous carbon frameworks, N2 adsorption-desorption tests were performed. In Fig. 5a, PCF-600 exhibits a low adsorption capacity, indicating that it does not have a well-developed pore structure, which agrees well with the SEM results. By comparison, the isotherms for both PCF-700 and PCF-800 show a typical type IV isotherm, i.e. there is major uptake in the low pressure region (P/P0 < 0.1) owing to the presence of micropores and an obvious hysteresis loop of type H3 (P/P0 = 0.4–0.90) because of the existence of a large amount of mesopores, indicating that some pore structure is introduced into the material at higher carbonization temperatures [53,54]. Besides that, a significant increment in the isotherms for PCF-700 and PCF-800 at the P/P0 above 0.90 indicates the possible presence of macropores in the carbon matrix. Fig. 5b illustrates the pore size distribution of porous carbon frameworks, which summarized by the following two features: (1) micropores with pore sizes about 1.5 nm which increase with increasing carbonization temperature; (2) mesopores mainly appear range of 3–10 nm. The corresponding textural characteristics of porous carbon frameworks are summarized in Table 3 . The specific surface area and pore volume of PCF-600 are significantly lower, measuring at 17.59 m2·g−1 and 0.0573 cm3·g−1, respectively. As a contrast, with increasing temperature, the specific surface area and total pore volume increase to 65.21 m2·g−1 and 0.1867 cm3·g−1 for PCF-700 and 121.13 m2·g−1 and 0.5358 cm3·g−1 for PCF-800, which may be due to the reorganization of smaller carbon nanosheets with surrounding carbon nanosheets, exposing more of the carbon nanosheets to the surface. In addition, the total pore volume and micropore volume also increased, probably because the small molecules within the pores are fully released at high temperatures. The porosity level of porous carbon frameworks is significantly affected by the carbonization temperature, as demonstrated by the results. The larger specific surface area and pore volume provide more surface area for electrode-electrolyte interactions, giving ample space and active sites for Li+ storage, and a meso/micropores hierarchical pore distribution facilitates the rapid transport of Li+, which contributes to improved electrochemical performance.

Fig. 6a displays the CV curves of the PCF-600 electrode recorded at a scan rate of 0.2 mV·s−1. During the first cathodic scan, a prominent peak at ca. 1.5 V and a weak peak at ca. 0.5 V are observed, corresponding to the insertion of Li+ into micropores and the formation of a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) films, respectively [55]. The peak observed at ca. 1.04 V during the anodic scan corresponds to the release of Li+ from the pore structure during the charging process. Furthermore, the SEI films and insertion/extraction redox reaction of Li+ are relatively stable after the initial cycle, which evidenced in the largely overlapping CV curves of the last two cycles. The stable SEI films are known to be effective in stopping the decomposition of electrolyte on the electrodes and reducing side reactions. By comparison, the CV curves of PCF-700 (Fig. 6b) and PCF-800 (Fig. 6c) are similar to PCF-600, indicating the same lithium storage mechanism. Fig. 6d-f show the GCD curves for the first three cycles of the three samples at 50 mA·g−1. The first charge/discharge capacities for the PCF-600, PCF-700, and PCF-800 anode are 384/809 mAh·g−1, 460/1021 mAh·g−1, and 331/706 mAh·g−1 respectively, and the relevant initial Coulombic efficiencies (CE) respectively reach 47.5 %, 45.1 %, and 46.9 %. It should be noted that PCF-700 exhibits a significantly higher initial discharge capacity of 1021 mAh·g−1 compared to onion-like carbon hollow nanostructures (OCHNs) [56] and high purity graphite (PG3) [57]. However, increasing the specific surface area did not result in any further increase in capacity. The decline in capacity of PCF-800, despite having a larger surface area, can be attributed to the excess carbon pores. This results in an increase in grain boundaries, leading to lower electrical conductivity. The formation of SEI films and the reaction of Li+ with structural defects are inevitable lead to the irreversible insertion of Li+ into the interior of the carbon group, resulting in a loss of capacity of porous carbon frameworks, which can also be noticed in other materials [58,59].

Fig. 7a. displays the rate capability of porous carbon frameworks across a range of current densities, from 50 to 5000 mA·g−1. At 50 mA·g−1, PCF-700 achieves a discharge specific capacity of 460 mAh·g−1, which stabilizes at 399 mAh·g−1. Even cycled at higher current densities of 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, and 5000 mA·g−1, it still exhibits high capacities of 319, 275, 219, 178, 115, and 72 mAh·g−1, respectively. Conversely, at the same rate, the PCF-600 sample delivers reversible capacities of 352, 287, 237, 180, 142, 99 and 35 mAh·g−1, and PCF-800 displays of 284, 324, 198, 149, 123, 87 and 16 mAh·g−1, individually. The excellent rate capacity of PCF-700 is mainly associated with its well-developed 3D porous frameworks, which provides abundant lithium storage sites, increases the electrode-electrolyte contact surfaces, shortens Li+ diffusion path, and possess the ability to accommodate the localized volume expansion of the carbon anode material. Most importantly, the specific capacity of PCF-700 can be restored to 404 mAh·g−1 by reducing the current density to 50 mA·g−1, which is slightly higher than the 399 mAh·g−1 observed in the previous 10 cycles performed under same conditions. The phenomenon is similar to those of nanostructured carbons including porous graphitic carbons [60], hollow carbon shell [61], and graphene-winged carbon nanotubes [62] as anode materials for LIBs.

Fig. 7b compares the cycle capability of anodes based on porous carbon frameworks at 100 mA·g−1. As expected, after 500 charge/discharge cycles, PCF-700 possess the highest specific capacity of 402 mAh·g−1, showing a satisfactory capacity retention of 91 %. In contrast, the residual capacities of PCF-600 and PCF-800 are 351 and 297 mAh·g−1, individually. Furthermore, the high reversible capacity with outstanding cycling stability for PCF-700 is much superior to those of reported carbon materials, which are summarized in Table S2 [63–67]. The outstanding cycling stability of PCF-700 is mostly because of the unique 3D morphology, which alleviates the volume changes that occur during the redox reactions of Li+ insertion/extraction, while also preserving the stability of the structure during repeated charge/discharge cycles. The long-term cycling performance of the PCF-700 is shown in Fig. 7c. Following 1000 cycles at 2000 mA·g−1, it exhibits a charge capacity of 273 mAh·g−1. Besides that, Coulombic efficiency of nearly 99 % for the PCF-700 electrode is observed after the initial several cycles. Interestingly, it is clearly observed that the reversible capacity of PCF-700 electrode gradually decreases in the first few cycles but turns to increase with cycling after several cycles, as the Coulombic efficiency becomes stable. The PCF-600 and PCF-800 electrodes also display similar capacity increment at 100 mA·g−1 and 2000 mA·g−1 with the cycling processes. The phenomenon is actually common in the porous carbons, and the increased capacity can be attributed to the activating process of the porous anode [68,69], where a variety of reactions occur, including electrode material activation and diffusion of Li+ into holes or defects of graphene etc.

To further study the electrochemical properties of materials, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out. Usually, all spectra show two depressed semicircles in the high and middle frequency region and an inclined line in the low frequency region. It has been reported that the semicircle in high frequency region represents interphase contact resistance (Rs), the semicircle in middle frequency region represents charge transfer resistance (Rct) and the inclined line is interpreted as resistance from diffusion of lithium ion (Wo) [70]. The electrochemical impedance parameters (Rs and Rct) of the samples listed in Table S3 are calculated by fitting the equivalent circuit in Fig. 7d. Clearly, the PCF-700 have the smallest Rs and Rct, which means that the PCF-700 anode presents more preferable electronic conductivity and Li+ transfer, resulting in an improved electrochemical performance. Moreover, the diffusion coefficient of Li+ is calculated as follows [71–73]:
(1)
D
Li +
=
R 2
T 2
2
A 2
n 4
F 4
C 2
σ 2

The aforementioned equation contains multiple parameters, including R that signifies the gas constant, T which represents the absolute temperature, A the contact area of the electrode, n the number of electrons transferred, F the Faraday constant, C the molar concentration of Li+, and σ that denotes the Warburg factor. Additionally, σ is related to Z′ through the corresponding equation:
(2)
Z '
=
R s
+
R ct
+
σω
− 1 / 2
Herein, Z' refers to the real component of the impedance spectrum in the low frequency range, Rs and Rct have been listed in Table S3, and ω denotes the angular frequency. As shown in Fig. 7e, σ represents the gradient of the fitted straight line Z' ∼ ω-1/2. As calculated by the formula, the Li+ diffusion coefficients of 2.25 × 10−16 cm2 s−1, 4.63 × 10−16 cm2 s−1 and 1.07 × 10−16 cm2 s−1 for the three electrodes, individually. The Li+ diffusion coefficient of PCF-700 is 2.1 and 4.3 times higher than those of PCF-600 and PCF-800, suggesting good Li+ transport properties of PCF-700.

Fig. 8a displays the CV curves obtained at different scan rates, which are utilized to perform a detailed analysis of the lithium storage mechanism of PCF-700. The correlation between peak current (i) and sweep frequency (ν) can be described by the following equation (Fig. 8b) [74,75]:
(3)
i =
aν b

In this equation, a represents a constant and b is the slope of the fitted line. (When b approaches 0.5, it indicates a diffusion-controlled contribution; when b approaches 1.0, it indicates the contribution of capacitive-effect, and when 0.5 < b < 1, it shows both diffusion-controlled and capacitive-effect.) The calculated b-value is calculated as 0.71 and 0.89 respectively, indicating that the lithium storage capacity as an anode material is contributed by both capacitive-effect and diffusion-controlled. In addition, the contribution of capacitive-effect to the lithium storage capacity can be assessed using the following formula:
(4)
i =
k 1
ν +
k 2
ν
1 / 2

In this equation, the currents generated by the contributions of capacitive-effect and diffusion-controlled are represented by k1ν and k2ν1/2, respectively. The red area in Fig. 8c represents the capacitive lithium storage capacity at 0.5 mV s−1, and Fig. 8d displays the capacitive-effect contribution and the diffusion-controlled contribution as a proportion of the total capacity at different sweep speeds. The contribution of capacitance shows a clear increase as the scanning rate is increased from 0.1 to 0.9 mV s−1, and the corresponding percentages of capacitive contribution are 41 %, 50 %, 54 %, 62 % and 67 %, respectively.

To study the structure changes during long cycling, the structure of PCF-700 after 500 cycles at 100 mA·g−1 was investigated using SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 9a, the morphology and structure of the PCF-700 keeps intact except that the outside surface becomes rough, probably because of the SEI formation and some residual electrolyte. The EDS elemental mappings also detected a distribution of P and F elements, corresponding to the composition of the SEI film covering the material surface of the LiPF6 salt-based electrolyte system (Fig. 9b) [3]. Moreover, the PCF-700 still remains a sheet-like morphology, revealing its morphology and structure are well preserved (Fig. 9c-d), thus stable electrode/material structure results in excellent electrochemical performances.

In general, coal tar pitch consists of many kinds of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, which can be easily tailoring to CQDs by mild chemical oxidation with H2O2. The resulting CQDs are homogeneous, stable, distinct crystallinity nature and rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, which can be used in aqueous systems. The porous carbon frameworks can be produced through carbonizing the CQDs powder at N2 atmosphere at elevated temperatures. The obtained porous carbon frameworks consist of cross-linked and twisted nanosheets, demonstrating desired specific surface area and porosity as anode for LIBs. Moreover, the unique 3D morphology alleviates the volume changes that occur during the redox reactions of Li+ insertion/extraction, while also preserving the stability of the structure. The anodes based on porous carbon frameworks display excellent long-term electrochemical stability and can achieve a maximum reversible capacity of 402 mAh·g−1 at 100 mA·g−1. Moreover, even after undergoing 1000 cycles at 2000 mA·g−1, it still retains a reversible capacity of 273 mAh·g−1, demonstrating its great potential for lithium storage applications. The encouraging results illustrate that the low cost of precursors and the simple fabrication process paves an alternative route for future production of anode materials in LIBs.
